document.title: M1 Lattice: Core Specification (Epoch 2)
document.purpose: Defines the structure and behavior of entities and their aspects in the M1 Lattice system.

core_principle: The M1 Lattice consists of a set of entities, each identified by a stable identity. All meaning, structure, and relationships are expressed through aspects attached to these entities.
design_principles: There is only one structural primitive: the entity.
Identity is stable; all meaning is expressed through aspects.
Links are entities recognized by a conventional link aspect.
Structure emerges through interpretation of aspects.
Ambiguity in the 'basic' aspect enables flexibility.
Precision is introduced through specialized aspects.

scope.description: The M1 Lattice specification defines the conceptual model of entities and aspects. It does not prescribe how these structures are implemented in memory or storage. Implementations may represent entities and aspects using any suitable data structures.

types.description: Defines the distinction between logical and semantic types in M1 specifications.
types.logical_type: The type as it appears in a JSON document (object, array, string, number, boolean, null). Implementations may further distinguish number into integer and float.
types.semantic_type: A more precise meaning within a logical type, describing how the value should be interpreted.

semantic_type_inventory.entity-id: A stable identifier for an entity, expressed as either a UUID (RFC 9562) or a Tag URI (RFC 4151).
semantic_type_inventory.resource-reference: A reference to a resource. It is recommended that this be expressed as an entity-id and resolved via tables. Direct references such as URLs or file paths are permitted.

rules: Logical types define storage format.
Semantic types define interpretation.
Semantic types are open and may be extended by implementations.
Semantic types not described in the inventory are intentionally left undefined and may be freely introduced and interpreted by implementations.

entity.description: The entity is the abstract representation of something.  It is uniquely identified by a stable identifier, which may be expressed as a UUID or Tag URI.  The chosen form becomes the permanent identifier of the entity.

aspect.description: An aspect is a named structure associated with an entity, representing a dimension of meaning, data, or behavior.

identity.description: Primary identifier for the aspect type (typically a Tag URI).

entity_id.description: Identifier of the entity to which this aspect applies.

contents.description: The structure of the aspect is determined by its identifier. No global schema is enforced.

rules: Aspects represent perspectives or dimensions of meaning associated with an entity.
Multiple aspects may exist for a single entity.
Aspects may represent incomplete or conflicting perspectives.
Interpretation of aspects is the responsibility of consuming systems.

basic.description: enables a baseline level of interaction with entities that would otherwise be indecipherable to a given system.
basic.note: The basic aspect provides a minimal, broadly interpretable layer of information to support generic browsing and interaction.

typehint.description: A short, human-readable indication of the entity's general nature. Examples include 'person', 'location', 'book', 'event', 'file', or 'link'. This field is informal and non-authoritative, and is intended to aid presentation, interpretation, and filtering.

name.description: A short, program-friendly identifier or label for the entity. Typically concise and stable, and may be used in code or compact displays.

title.description: A human-readable title for the entity, suitable for display in user interfaces.

description.description: A textual explanation of the entity, providing context, meaning, or interpretation. Typically a paragraph long, but could be empty, or could be as long as an essay.

tags.description: A list of classification labels used to group or categorize the entity.

url.description: A URL associated with the entity, typically pointing to an external resource or canonical reference.
url.example: If the entity is Amazon (the company), the URL entry would likely be 'https://amazon.com'. For a web page bookmark, the url would be the URL of the bookmark.

file.description: Reference to a file resource associated with this entity.

image.description: Reference to an image resource associated with this entity.

date.description: A general-purpose date associated with the entity. Interpretation is context-dependent.

location.description: A general-purpose location associated with the entity. Interpretation is context-dependent.

notes.description: Free-form notes or additional information about the entity. Intended for informal or unstructured content.

hook.description: A concise, attention-directing expression, often a phrase, intended to quickly convey the entity's significance or invite engagement.

typing_guidance: The 'typehint' field provides a human-readable indication of an entity's general nature.
For entities representing relationships, 'link' is typically used, with more specific relationship semantics defined within the link aspect.
More specific or structured typing should be expressed through aspects.
rules: These fields are optional and loosely interpreted.
They are intended for general usability and human readability.
They are not authoritative or strictly defined.
More precise structure should be expressed through specialized aspects.

link.description: Links are expressed as entities that contain a link aspect.

type.description: Optional identifier of an entity describing the relationship type.

rules: Links are entities with their own identity.
A link is recognized by the presence of the link aspect.
Links are directional, expressed from a source entity to a target entity.
Multiple links may exist between the same pair of entities.
Links may include additional aspects beyond the link aspect.
Links do not have special status in the core model; they are interpreted by convention.

log.description: Logs on entities preserve a common log of activities.

timestamp.description: the timestamp for this log note

event.description: A logged event code. Values are user-defined, but convergence on shared vocabularies is recommended.

vocabulary.CREATED: The entity was created at this time (as reified M1 data).
vocabulary.UPDATED: The entity's data was meaningfully updated at this time.
vocabulary.ACCESSED: The entity was notably accessed at this time.
vocabulary.DEPRECATED: Use of this entity is discouraged after this time.
vocabulary.NOTE: A textual note was written into the log at this time.

note.description: Human-readable note associated with this log entry.

agent.description: Identifier of an entity representing the originator of the action: any entity acting with intent or effect, such as a person, organization, or artificial intelligence system.

via.description: Identifier of an entity representing the mechanism, tool, or activity through which the action was carried out.

rules: Log entries are ordered chronologically.
Logs may merge; entries should endure.
Entries should be appended; existing entries should not be modified.
Multiple logs from different sources may coexist.
Interpretation of events is the responsibility of consuming systems.
Log entries may be combined from multiple sources into a single log. Individual entries should be preserved and not collapsed.
Duplicate entries may be eliminated during merging if they are identical.

tables.description: Tables provide a mapping from identifiers to resource locations. They are not part of the M1 Lattice ontology, but serve as a local resolution layer connecting entities to concrete resources such as files or URLs.

<identifier>.description: A list of locations where a resource matching the referenced identifier may be found.

type.description: The type of location. Common values include 'file' and 'url', but the set of valid types is open and may be extended.

path.description: A local filesystem path. Required when type is 'file'.

url.description: A string representing the location value, such as a URL. Required when type is 'url'.

rules: Tables map identifiers (entity ids) to zero or more resource locations.  Identifiers may be expressed as UUIDs or Tag URIs.
Multiple locations per identifier are allowed and expected.
Tables are environment-specific and may differ across systems.
The M1 Lattice specification does not define how a location is selected; this is the responsibility of consuming applications.
The set of location types is open; implementations may define additional types beyond 'file' and 'url'.
Tables are not required for operation of the lattice, but provide a mechanism for resolving entities to concrete resources.
